citations.narrative:
The facility continues to exceed the maximum number of animals allowed under their license. The facility is approved for
up to 100 animals, but 132 animals were documented during the inspection. The USDA license authorizes a specific
number of animals to ensure that the facility and personnel possess adequate facilities, employees and education to
provide sufficient husbandry to the regulated animals.
A licensee must obtain a new license before any change resulting in more than the authorized number of animals on hand
at any single point in time during the period of licensure. The licensee must reapply for a license that properly reflects the
number of regulated animals under the license or reduce the number of regulated animals to a number which is under the
maximum threshold authorized under the current license.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
At the time of the inspection, the Attending Veterinarian (AV) had not conducted a site visit. The facility's written program
of veterinary care indicates that AV visits are to occur "biannually or annually". However, a facility representative stated
that the veterinarian had not visited the premises within the past18 months. Routine veterinary site visits are required to
provide the professional guidance necessary to optimize the health and welfare of the animals housed at each site.
Each dealer and exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time
attending veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary
care and regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the dealer or exhibitor.

The record of animals on hand (inventory) was not current. The following animals were missing from the inventory: three
emus, five goats, and a rabbit born on the premises and the acquisition two pigs. The dates of death for two golden
crowned cranes, one rose-ringed parakeet were also not recorded. Complete and accurate records are necessary to
facilitate the tracking of animals and maintain compliance with other aspects of the AWA. The licensee shall make, keep,
and maintain records or forms which fully disclose the information concerning animals, purchased or otherwise acquired,
owned, held, leased, or otherwise in his or her possession or under his or her control, or which is transported, sold,
euthanized, or otherwise disposed of by that dealer or exhibitor. This record must be kept current and maintained to
accurately reflect all the animals. Correct by February 18,2026.

The facility, which houses primates in outdoor enclosures, continues to lack an adequate perimeter fence. Multiple areas
of the perimeter fence continue to be less than 6 feet tall. An insufficient perimeter fence can allow unwanted animals or
people to enter the facility and potentially harm the animals within.
The perimeter fence must be constructed so that it protects nonhuman primates by restricting unauthorized humans, and
animals the size of dogs, skunks, and raccoons from going through it or under it and having contact with the nonhuman
primates. It must be of sufficient height (at least six feet high) to keep unwanted species out. It must be of sufficient
distance (at least three feet) from the outside wall or fence of the primary enclosure to prevent physical contact between
animals inside the enclosure and outside the perimeter fence.

The facility, which houses a sloth, genets, kangaroos, wallabies, kinkajous and porcupines in outdoor enclosures,
continues to lack an adequate perimeter fence. Most of the premises continues to be surrounded by a perimeter fence
that is less than 6 feet tall. A perimeter fence protects animals from contact with unauthorized people and animals. A
perimeter fence also acts as a secondary containment system if an animal escapes from its primary enclosure.
An outdoor housing facility must be enclosed by a perimeter fence that serves as an effective barrier to protect animals in
the facility from unauthorized persons and animals. Fences must be at least 6 feet high unless approved in writing by the
Administrator. Additionally, fences must be at least 3 feet in distance from the outside wall or fence of the primary
enclosure to prevent physical contract between animals inside the enclosure and outside the perimeter fence.

An enclosure housing a pig and calf contained an excessive amount of feces, covering the majority of the stall area.
There was also a large accumulation of fecal material in the outdoor enclosure housing two albino porcupines. Feces
accumulation attracts pests and increases the likelihood of disease for the animals. Excreta shall be removed from
primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize
disease hazards and to reduce odors. Correct by February 11, 2026.

At the time of the inspection, there were two employees present to feed animals and clean enclosures for 132 regulated
animals. A facility representative stated that two employees recently resigned and that the facility is currently in the
process of hiring additional staff.
The non-compliances identified during this inspection, as well as those identified on previous inspection reports, are
consistent with inadequate staffing levels. Without a sufficient number of adequately trained employees, the facility cannot
provide the husbandry practices necessary to maintain the animals in a manner that ensures their health and welfare.
A sufficient number of adequately trained employees shall be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level of
husbandry practices set forth in this subpart. Such practices shall be under a supervisor who has a background in animal
care. Correct by March 10, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.1(b)(2)(i)  Repeat
   
Requirements and application 
The facility continues to exceed the maximum number of animals allowed under their license. The facility is approved for 
up to 100 animals, but 132 animals were documented during the inspection. The USDA license authorizes a specific

at any single point in time during the period of licensure. The licensee must reapply for a license that properly reflects the 
number of regulated animals under the license or reduce the number of regulated animals to a number which is under the 
maximum threshold authorized under the current license. 
2.40(a)(1)  Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
At the time of the inspection, the Attending Veterinarian (AV) had not conducted a site visit. The facility's written program 
of veterinary care indicates that AV visits are to occur "biannually or annually". However, a facility representative stated

Each dealer and exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time 
attending veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary 
care and regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the dealer or exhibitor. 
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
The record of animals on hand (inventory) was not current. The following animals were missing from the inventory: three 
emus, five goats, and a rabbit born on the premises and the acquisition two pigs. The dates of death for two golden

and maintain records or forms which fully disclose the information concerning animals, purchased or otherwise acquired,

euthanized, or otherwise disposed of by that dealer or exhibitor. This record must be kept current and maintained to 
accurately reflect all the animals. Correct by February 18,2026. 
3.78(d)  Repeat
   
Outdoor housing facilities. 
The facility, which houses primates in outdoor enclosures, continues to lack an adequate perimeter fence. Multiple areas 
of the perimeter fence continue to be less than 6 feet tall. An insufficient perimeter fence can allow unwanted animals or 
people to enter the facility and potentially harm the animals within.  
 
The perimeter fence must be constructed so that it protects nonhuman primates by restricting unauthorized humans, and

primates. It must be of sufficient height (at least six feet high) to keep unwanted species out. It must be of sufficient 
distance (at least three feet) from the outside wall or fence of the primary enclosure to prevent physical contact between 
animals inside the enclosure and outside the perimeter fence. 
3.127(d)  Repeat
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
The facility, which houses a sloth, genets, kangaroos, wallabies, kinkajous and porcupines in outdoor enclosures, 
continues to lack an adequate perimeter fence. Most of the premises continues to be surrounded by a perimeter fence 
that is less than 6 feet tall. A perimeter fence protects animals from contact with unauthorized people and animals. A 
perimeter fence also acts as a secondary containment system if an animal escapes from its primary enclosure. 
 
An outdoor housing facility must be enclosed by a perimeter fence that serves as an effective barrier to protect animals in

Administrator. Additionally, fences must be at least 3 feet in distance from the outside wall or fence of the primary 
enclosure to prevent physical contract between animals inside the enclosure and outside the perimeter fence. 
3.131(a) 
   
Sanitation. 
An enclosure housing a pig and calf contained an excessive amount of feces, covering the majority of the stall area. 
There was also a large accumulation of fecal material in the outdoor enclosure housing two albino porcupines. Feces 
accumulation attracts pests and increases the likelihood of disease for the animals. Excreta shall be removed from 
primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize

3.132 
   
Employees.

At the time of the inspection, there were two employees present to feed animals and clean enclosures for 132 regulated 
animals. A facility representative stated that two employees recently resigned and that the facility is currently in the 
process of hiring additional staff.  
 
The non-compliances identified during this inspection, as well as those identified on previous inspection reports, are 
consistent with inadequate staffing levels. Without a sufficient number of adequately trained employees, the facility cannot 
provide the husbandry practices necessary to maintain the animals in a manner that ensures their health and welfare. 
 
A sufficient number of adequately trained employees shall be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level of 
husbandry practices set forth in this subpart. Such practices shall be under a supervisor who has a background in animal 
care. Correct by March 10, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
